article_title: ChatGPT Uninstalls Surge 295% After OpenAI Signs Pentagon Deal

findings.message: This article was requested by a human editor — apply heightened scrutiny to content accuracy and source quality

content_preview.title: ChatGPT Uninstalls Surge 295% After OpenAI Signs Pentagon Deal
content_preview.summary: Consumer backlash follows OpenAI's DoD agreement; Claude climbs to No. 1 on U.S. App Store as QuitGPT campaign claims 1.5 million participants.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

ChatGPT mobile app uninstalls surged 295% in the United States following OpenAI's announcement of a deal with the Department of Defense, as consumers expressed alarm over artificial intelligence being deployed in classified military environments. The backlash represents one of the sharpest consumer reactions to an AI company's government partnership on record, with rival Anthropic's Claude app climbing to the top of the U.S. App Store in the days that followed.

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured 809-word News piece with clear four-section organization (Pentagon Deal, Consumer Backlash, Competitor Gains, Amended Contract) plus a What We Don't Know section that appropriately caveats uncertainties. The Analysis section is balanced and avoids editorializing.
editor_notes.source_verification: Six sources verified: TechCrunch (×2, covering separate aspects — uninstall data and deal details), Fortune, CNBC, NBC News, Euronews. All outlets are reputable and within the allowlist. Minor source concentration with two TechCrunch articles, but they cover distinct factual claims.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Key claims verified against research: 295% uninstall spike (TechCrunch exclusive), 9% typical daily uninstall baseline, 775% surge in 1-star reviews (+100% Sunday), Claude reaching #1 U.S. App Store, Claude downloads +37%/+51% day-over-day, QuitGPT 1.5M self-reported participants (Euronews), Anthropic supply-chain risk designation (multiple sources), Brad Carson quote (NBC News, named credentialed source). Surveillance clause language quoted from NBC News matches reporting. Altman's 'looked opportunistic and sloppy' attributed to CNBC per reporting.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. The automated 'loaded language' warning fires on the word 'definitely' — this is a false positive: the word appears exclusively within a directly quoted Altman statement ('was definitely rushed'), not in editorial prose. No AI self-reference, no sensationalism.
editor_notes.originality: No prior coverage of this topic in the archive. Unique and timely.
editor_notes.human_requested_scrutiny: Heightened scrutiny applied as required. The article maintains neutrality despite the inherently controversial subject matter — it presents OpenAI's defense of the deal, the consumer backlash, legal experts' skepticism, and the competitor gains without adopting the frame of either side. The QuitGPT campaign's figures are accurately presented as self-reported claims, not verified data. The 'What We Don't Know' section actively flags limitations in the uninstall data source and the QuitGPT participant methodology.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, timely submission on a significant AI industry story. All integrity checks pass, sources are reputable and correctly attributed, tone is neutral, and the article handles a politically charged topic with appropriate restraint. Ready for publication.
